17: YHWH eloheicha bekirbecha gibbor yoshi'a yasis alayich besimcha yacharish be'ahavato yagel alayich berinah: 'The LORD your God is in your midst, a mighty one who will save; he will rejoice over you with gladness; he will quiet you by his love; he will exult over you with loud singing.' This verse is the OT's most intimate statement of divine delight in his people: YHWH not merely tolerating but actively rejoicing over Israel, singing over her. The father-over-child image (quieting, singing) is the most tender description of YHWH's covenant love. The NT fulfillment: 'The Father himself loves you' (John 16:27); 'God is love' (1 John 4:8); 'rejoice in the Lord always' (Philippians 4:4) — the joy is mutual, as Zephaniah's vision shows: YHWH singing over his people is the grounding for the people's own joy.
1: hoi hamorea venigga'ala ha'ir hayonah: 'Woe to her who is rebellious and defiled, the oppressing city!' The sudden shift from the Gentile nations (Zephaniah 2) to Jerusalem (ha'ir) is the prophetic masterstroke of the book. The three characterizations: morea (rebellious, from mara, H4784 — to be defiant against YHWH), nig'ala (defiled, H1351 — unclean, polluted), ha-yonah (the oppressing city, from yanah, H3238 — to oppress, tyrannize). Jerusalem is more culpable than the surrounding nations because she has greater revelation and covenant privilege. The woe oracle that condemned the Gentile nations now turns inward on God's own city.
2: lo shama'ah beqol lo laqqechah musar bADONAI lo vatachah el Eloheiha lo qarevah: 'She listens to no voice; she accepts no correction; she does not trust in the LORD; she does not draw near to her God.' The four negative characterizations are all relational failures: she does not hear, she does not receive discipline (musar), she does not trust (vatach), she does not draw near (qarev). These are not external behavioral failures but covenant-relationship failures: hearing, discipline, trust, and nearness are the four dimensions of covenant faithfulness. Jerusalem's failure is comprehensive.
3: sareha beqirbah arayot sho'agim shopeteha ze'evei erev lo garemu laboqer: 'Her officials within her are roaring lions; her judges are evening wolves that leave nothing till the morning.' The ruling class (sarim) and the judiciary (shopetim) are compared to predatory animals: lions (arayot) that roar as they devour, and evening wolves (ze'evei erev) that consume everything before dawn. The wolves that leave nothing till morning are so voracious they consume the night's victims entirely before light comes. The social order that should protect the vulnerable has become the instrument of their consumption (cf. Ezekiel 22:25-27, the same animal imagery for Jerusalem's ruling class).
4: nevieiha pachazim anshei mirmah kohaneihah chilellu qodesh chamesu Torah: 'Her prophets are fickle, treacherous men; her priests profane what is holy; they do violence to the law.' The two remaining authority groups — prophets and priests — are equally corrupt. The prophets are pachazim (H6348 — reckless, impudent) and anshei mirmah (treacherous men). The priests chilellu qodesh (profane the holy, H2490) and chamesu Torah (do violence to the Torah, H2554 — to wrong, oppress). This four-fold corruption of governing structures — officials, judges, prophets, priests — means the entire social fabric of covenant life has collapsed from the top down.
5: YHWH tzaddiq beqirbah lo ya'aseh avlah baboqer baboqer mishpato yiten la'or lo ne'dar: 'The LORD within her is righteous; he does no injustice; every morning he brings his justice to light; he does not fail.' YHWH's righteousness (tzaddiq, H6662) in the midst of Jerusalem's unrighteous city is the theological contrast at the center of Zephaniah 3. Every morning (baboqer baboqer, the doubled noun for emphasis) he brings his justice to light — consistent, reliable, unfailing. The contrast with the corrupt officials/judges/prophets/priests could not be sharper: while the rulers consume and deceive, YHWH faithfully administers justice each day. Yet the unrighteous know no shame.
6: hikhratti goyim nashamu pinnoteichem hechravti chutzoteichem mibaliy over nikhresu areichem me'ein yoshev me'ein adam: 'I have cut off nations; their battlements are in ruins; I have laid waste their streets so that no one walks in them; their cities have been made desolate.' YHWH argues from the lesson of history: he has already destroyed nations (hikhratti goyim) — the Assyrian destructions of neighboring states were divine object lessons. The battlements, streets, cities reduced to emptiness: Jerusalem should have learned from what happened to other peoples. Divine judgment on others is intended as a warning to the observer (cf. Luke 13:1-5).
7: amarti ach tir'i oti tiqqichi musar velo yikaret me'onah kol asher paqadti aleha: 'I said, Surely you will fear me; you will accept correction; then your dwelling will not be cut off.' The hope implicit in divine judgment: correction should produce fear of YHWH and willingness to receive musar (H4148, discipline, instruction). 'But all the more they were eager to make all their deeds corrupt' (ashkimu hikshitu kol aliloteichem) — the purpose of corrective judgment was frustrated by increased corruption. The intensification of sin in response to divine correction is the prophetic diagnosis of incurable covenant violation (cf. Jeremiah 5:3; Amos 4:6-11).
8: lachen chakku li neum YHWH leyom qumi le'ad ki mishpati le'esof goyim liqbetzi mamlakhot lishpoch aleihem za'ami kol charon api: 'Therefore wait for me, declares the LORD, for the day when I rise up to seize the prey. For my decision is to gather nations, to assemble kingdoms, to pour out upon them my indignation, all my burning anger.' The command chakku (wait, H2442 — to wait with longing) is given to the faithful remnant: wait for my day of vindication. YHWH will gather all nations for judgment — the universal scope connects to Zephaniah 1:2-3 and the final judgment of Revelation 16:14-16.
9: ki az ahafoch el amim safah verurah liqro kulam beshem YHWH le'ovdo shekhem echad: 'For at that time I will change the peoples to a pure language, that all of them may call upon the name of the LORD and serve him with one accord.' The reversal of Babel is the eschatological vision: at Babel, YHWH confused the languages (balal, Genesis 11:7-9); now he will give a pure/clear lip (safah verurah = pure speech). The result: all peoples calling on the name of YHWH in unity (shekhem echad, one shoulder = unanimously). The NT fulfillment is Pentecost (Acts 2:1-11) — miraculous speech in all languages as the reversal of Babel — and ultimately Revelation 7:9-10 (multitude from every language worshiping the Lamb).
10: me'ever lenaharei Kush atriday bat pitzotai yovilu minchati: 'From beyond the rivers of Cush my worshipers, the daughter of my dispersed ones, shall bring my offering.' Kush (H3568 — Ethiopia/Nubia) is the most distant land known to the ancient Near Eastern world; 'beyond the rivers of Cush' means the furthest reaches of the inhabited earth. From there, YHWH's scattered worshipers (pitzotai, my dispersed ones) will bring offerings. The ingathering is universal: from the most remote regions of the earth. The New Exodus of returning exiles expands to include Gentile worshipers from all directions — the missionary eschatology underlying the NT's Great Commission (Matthew 28:19).
11: bayom hahu lo tevoshi mikol alilotayich asher pasha't bi ki az asir miqqirbech alilei ga'avatech: 'On that day you shall not be put to shame because of the deeds by which you have rebelled against me; for then I will remove from your midst your proudly exultant ones.' The removal of alilei ga'avatech (the proudly exultant ones) from the remnant community is the purification that makes restoration possible. The holy mountain (har qodshi) will be inhabited only by those who are genuinely humble before YHWH. The remnant will not be ashamed because the sources of shame — the corrupt leaders and the proud — will be removed.
12: vehi'artiy bekirbech am oni vadal vechasu beshem YHWH: 'But I will leave in your midst a people humble and lowly. They shall seek refuge in the name of the LORD.' The remnant theology reaches its theological peak: YHWH will leave (vehishartiy) a people who are ani vedat (humble/afflicted and poor/lowly). The words aniyim (H6041) and dalim (H1800) describe the marginalized — not the powerful who hold religious-political structures, but the meek who rely entirely on YHWH. These are the aniyim of the Psalms (Psalms 9:12, 18; Zephaniah 10:12, 17) and the ptochoi (poor) of Jesus's beatitude (Matthew 5:3). Their characteristic action: seeking refuge in the name of YHWH (chasah beshem YHWH).
13: she'erit Yisrael lo ya'asu avlah velo yedabberu kazav velo yimmatze bepiyhem leshon tarmit: 'The remnant of Israel shall do no injustice and speak no lies; nor shall there be found in their mouth a deceitful tongue.' The remnant (she'erit Yisrael) is characterized by three ethical negatives: no injustice (avlah), no lies (kazav), no deceit in the mouth (leshon tarmit). The mouth/tongue emphasis connects to the Babel reversal of v9: pure lips/speech. The remnant's purity is first ethical before cultic: they are truthful in their dealings. 'They shall graze and lie down, and none shall make them afraid' — the pastoral image of complete security, the opposite of the predatory rulers of v3.
14: roni bat tziyon hari'i Yisrael simchi vealzi bekhol lev bat Yerushalayim: 'Sing aloud, O daughter of Zion; shout, O Israel! Rejoice and exult with all your heart, O daughter of Jerusalem!' The final section (vv14-20) is a sustained call to joy — the complete reversal of the lament. The four-fold address (daughter of Zion, Israel, daughter of Jerusalem) creates a chorus of exhortation. The verbs: roni (shout for joy), hari'i (shout), simchi (rejoice), alzi (exult) — all indicating intense joyful celebration. The ground: YHWH has removed the judgments (v15) and is in the midst of his people (v17). The angel's greeting to Mary (Luke 1:28: Rejoice, highly favored one) echoes this verse.
15: hesir YHWH mishpatayich pinnah oyevech melech Yisrael YHWH beqirbech lo tiyri ra od: 'The LORD has taken away the judgments against you; he has cleared away your enemies. The King of Israel, the LORD, is in your midst; you shall never again fear evil.' The ground of joy is fourfold: (1) judgments removed (hesir mishpatayich), (2) enemies cleared (pinnah oyevech), (3) YHWH as King present in the midst (melech Yisrael YHWH beqirbech), (4) no more evil to fear. The title melech Yisrael combined with beqirbech (in your midst) is the culmination of Immanuel theology: YHWH present with his people as reigning king. NT: Jesus as King of Israel (John 1:49) present through the Spirit (John 14:17).
16: bayom hahu ye'amer liYerushalayim al tir'i Tziyon al yirpu yadayich: 'On that day it shall be said to Jerusalem: Fear not, O Zion; let not your hands grow weak.' The phrase 'fear not' (al tir'i) is the divine comfort formula throughout Isaiah's Servant Songs (Isaiah 40:9; Zephaniah 41:10, 13, 14; Zephaniah 43:1, 5; Zephaniah 44:2, 8) — YHWH's direct word of reassurance to those in distress. 'Let not your hands grow weak' (al yirpu yadayich) — limp/slack hands are the OT image of paralysis and defeat (2 Samuel 4:1; Isaiah 35:3; Jeremiah 6:24; Hebrews 12:12). The day of YHWH's presence makes human fear and weakness inappropriate.
18: nugei mimmo'ed asafti mimmekh hayah masa aleiha cherpah: 'I will gather those of you who mourn for the festival, so that you will no longer suffer reproach.' Those who mourn for the appointed feasts (nugei mimmo'ed) — the exiles or the oppressed who cannot celebrate the covenant festivals — will be gathered. The reproach (cherpah) that clung to them (inability to worship, exile's shame, the taunts of enemies) will be lifted. The gathering for festival worship is the positive eschatological vision: YHWH's people celebrating his presence in his appointed times, freed from every impediment.
19: hineni oseh et kol manaiyich baaet hahi vehosha'ti et hatzolea vehannidachah eqabbetz: 'Behold, at that time I will deal with all your oppressors. And I will save the lame and gather the outcast, and I will change their shame into praise and renown in all the earth.' YHWH's personal intervention (hineni = behold, I myself) brings triple transformation: the oppressors are dealt with, the vulnerable are rescued (hatzolea = the lame; hannidachah = the driven-out/outcast), and shame becomes praise (cherpah levushmah). The lame and outcast who are gathered echoes Jesus's parable of the great banquet (Luke 14:21: bring in the poor and crippled and blind and lame).
20: baaet hahi avi etchem uvaaet qabbetzi etchem ki ettem liShem veliShem bekhol amei ha'aretz: 'At that time I will bring you in, at the time when I gather you together; for I will make you renowned and praised among all the peoples of the earth, when I restore your fortunes before your eyes, says the LORD.' The book's final verse gathers all the restoration themes: the bringing-in (avi etchem, the Exodus return), the gathering (qabetz), and the renown (shem utehillah, name and praise). Shem (name/renown) before all peoples is the reversal of the exile's shame (cherpah): the people who were the derision of nations become their object of wonder. The final word is neum YHWH (the oracle of YHWH) — sealed by divine authority as the last word of the book.
